output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Type confusion leading to stack out-of-bounds read in `damon_stat_set_monitoring_region`.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"In `damon_stat_set_monitoring_region()`, `damon_set_regions()` is invoked as:
`return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, addr_unit, min_region_sz);`

The third parameter of `damon_set_regions()` is `unsigned int nr_ranges`, which dictates the number of elements in the `ranges` array. The patch erroneously passes the physical address scale factor (`addr_unit`) instead of the actual array length (which should be `1`, as `&addr_range` is a pointer to a single local stack variable).

If `addr_unit` is configured or modified to be greater than 1, `damon_set_regions()` will interpret it as the array length and iterate past the bounds of the single-element `addr_range` on the stack. This will result in an out-of-bounds stack read, treating uninitialized stack memory as physical address ranges."},{"problem":"Architectural flaw creating a single bounding box that includes non-RAM holes, leading to skewed bandwidth statistics.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"The `damon_stat_system_ram_walk_fn` callback records the start of the first System RAM resource and the end of the last System RAM resource, creating a single contiguous bounding box `[first.start, last.end]`.

On systems with sparse physical memory topologies (like NUMA architectures), this unified region will encompass all massive non-RAM physical address holes (e.g., PCI MMIO) residing between valid RAM nodes. When DAMON dynamically monitors this region, a sub-region may overlap both a valid RAM segment and a massive physical hole. Because the estimated bandwidth computation (`access_bytes += (r->ar.end - r->ar.start) * r->nr_accesses`) multiplies observed accesses by the region's overall size, the inclusion of the hole artificially inflates the region size, drastically skewing and corrupting the bandwidth statistics. The correct approach is to dynamically collect an array of disjoint `struct damon_addr_range` elements (one for each RAM block) and pass the array to `damon_set_regions()`."},{"problem":"Potential integer overflow in end address calculation.","severity":"Low","severity_explanation":"In `damon_stat_set_monitoring_region()`, the region's end address is calculated as:
`addr_range.end = damon_stat_res_to_core_addr(arg.res.end + 1, addr_unit);`

If the physical memory layout extends to the absolute maximum value representable by `resource_size_t` (e.g., `0xFFFFFFFF` on a 32-bit architecture without PAE), adding `1` to `arg.res.end` will wrap around to `0`. This overflow causes the computed end address to evaluate to `0`, resulting in an invalid address range (`start >= end`) and effectively breaking the monitoring setup for that machine."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit 4b36180a519af35831edc0f0d2c23cbb69fd0eab
Author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

mm/damon/stat: monitor all System RAM resources

This commit updates damon_stat to monitor all System RAM instead of just the
biggest contiguous resource. This addresses an issue on NUMA systems where
only a small fraction of physical memory was being monitored.

> diff --git a/mm/damon/stat.c b/mm/damon/stat.c
> index f9a2028483b0..3ed71db33e89 100644
> --- a/mm/damon/stat.c
> +++ b/mm/damon/stat.c
> @@ -145,12 +145,57 @@ static int damon_stat_damon_call_fn(void *data)
[ ... ]
> +struct damon_stat_system_ram_range_walk_arg {
> +	bool walked;
> +	struct resource res;
> +};
> +
> +static int damon_stat_system_ram_walk_fn(struct resource *res, void *arg)
> +{
> +	struct damon_stat_system_ram_range_walk_arg *a = arg;
> +
> +	if (!a->walked) {
> +		a->walked = true;
> +		a->res.start = res->start;
> +	}
> +	a->res.end = res->end;

Will this create a single bounding box that includes non-RAM holes?

By recording the start of the first System RAM resource and the end of the
last, this unified region encompasses all non-RAM physical address holes
(like PCI MMIO) residing between valid RAM nodes. When DAMON dynamically
monitors this region, a sub-region may overlap both a valid RAM segment
and a physical hole.

Because the estimated bandwidth computation multiplies observed accesses
by the region's overall size, the inclusion of the hole artificially
inflates the region size, drastically skewing and corrupting the bandwidth
statistics.

Should this instead dynamically collect an array of disjoint ranges (one
for each RAM block) and pass the array to damon_set_regions()?

> +	return 0;
> +}
[ ... ]
> +static int damon_stat_set_monitoring_region(struct damon_target *t,
> +		unsigned long addr_unit, unsigned long min_region_sz)
> +{
> +	struct damon_addr_range addr_range;
> +	struct damon_stat_system_ram_range_walk_arg arg = {};
> +
> +	walk_system_ram_res(0, -1, &arg, damon_stat_system_ram_walk_fn);
> +	if (!arg.walked)
> +		return -EINVAL;
> +	addr_range.start = damon_stat_res_to_core_addr(
> +			arg.res.start, addr_unit);
> +	addr_range.end = damon_stat_res_to_core_addr(
> +			arg.res.end + 1, addr_unit);

Can arg.res.end + 1 overflow here?

If the physical memory layout extends to the absolute maximum value
representable by resource_size_t (such as 0xFFFFFFFF on a 32-bit
architecture), adding 1 will wrap around to 0. This overflow causes the
computed end address to evaluate to 0, resulting in an invalid address
range.

> +	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, addr_unit, min_region_sz);

Does this pass the physical address scale factor instead of the actual array
length?

The third parameter of damon_set_regions() is unsigned int nr_ranges,
which dictates the number of elements in the ranges array. Here, addr_unit
is passed instead.

If addr_unit is configured or modified to be greater than 1,
damon_set_regions() will interpret it as the array length and iterate past
the bounds of the single-element addr_range on the stack, treating
uninitialized stack memory as physical address ranges.

> +}
[ ... ]

"}
